speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- no. And that, that, we-- I was working in the camp there. And then it took a while. We were very impatient. We wanted to come to America. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your sister go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she went. She went. And I was there with my brother-in-law and then some cousins who survived, from another city. They joined us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were in the DP camp together. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then I heard that if you were in a children's home, you don't have to wait for a visa. So I said, since I qualify, might as well. And I went to inquire about it. And I got myself checked into the, to the children's home. And I remember the first evening, I came down to the kitchen. And they were serving dinner. And I said, I'm kosher. And they said, we don't have kosher. I was in this very stubborn. I was brought up a certain way. And it was instilled in me in such a manner that I liked being who I was. So I, I said, no, I'm not going to eat it. And they got very upset with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a, an American lady who said to me, what's your problem? Your, your father was a rabbi? And I said, no. He was just a very religious man. I was brought up this way. And I said, I don't want to eat non-kosher.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, but, to save your life, you're allowed. And I argued the point. I said, yes, if you're in danger. But now I'm free. So, you know, I can eat what I want. And-- because the rule, of course, is that if it's a matter of saving your life, you can eat anything. And we did during that year.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they got me a little pot. And they cooked potatoes and carrots and whatever. And I, I made it. And then I came to the United States. When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- in, in November. I arrived November 10, 1947. And they took us to a large building in the Bronx, in New York. And then I had an interesting experience. I don't know how interesting it is for this story, but just to end the story on a good note. There was a, a recreation room. And I was playing ping-pong with someone. And I saw, in the doorway, a nice looking man, just standing in the doorway and looking in. And then the woman in charge there called me out. And she said, there is a man here who wants to adopt you. He heard there is a ship came in with, you know, orphans, children that went through the camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and here, I get another chuckle. Because I took a look. And I said, well, he's not wearing a hat. [LAUGHS] So he's not so religious. And she said, are you crazy? You have, [LAUGHS] you have an opportunity, this man.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, you go outside and look at that beautiful car he arrived with. In 1947, having a beautiful car, you know, you had to have money. And he can give you this education. And he, you know, has two daughters. He's married with two daughters. And every opportunity, I mean, it's a dream come true. And I said, well, I don't think so. But my uncle is coming to visit. And I'll talk to him. And my uncle came. And he really lived on the Lower East Side in Manhattan, which was pretty run-down. And he wasn't a wealthy man. And they didn't live in luxury. But he was very special. And he was my mother's brother. And I said, uncle-- of course we-- I didn't remember him. I said, this and this happened. You know, this man came. And I'm told he will do such wonderful things for me. And so I remember my uncle saying, I will not tell you what to do. I can only say that it will be my pleasure to have you. And-- but if you make a decision where you think you could have a better life, you know, but I will let you decide. And at that moment, I decided to go with my uncle. And he always was-- he was just a second father to me, just a very wonderful person. Treated me like his own daughter, and was very, very special. And so then I got married and had children, and all that. So really, I don't know how relevant it is to anything else. And by the way, I did mention that I went to Brooklyn College and Queens College. And I don't want you to think I got so many degrees. I just went whenever I had an opportunity, and just went to, hopefully, to, to study. And then I had a child. And then I couldn't make it. So I did get some of the education. But I cannot say that I'm a college grad. Anybody who will listen to me-- I don't want to tell a lie. But I did get married, and three wonderful children, and grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Renee, what, of all the experiences that you had, what do you think allowed you-- what quality in you allowed you to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, again, going back to religion, we always feel that it's [INAUDIBLE], which it was meant to be. And you know, that it wasn't in your own power is how we see it. You couldn't say, well, I'm going to make it because I'm going to deal with it in a certain way. And I will survive. If it's it's like, you would think today of, of these illness-- terminal illness, you know. You can be strong. And you can be healthy. And, and you still-- you can eat healthy. And you can exercise. And it will still happen if it has to happen. So we believe in that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I think that in the way my life went during that time and the way I was surviving maybe a little better or a little easier, was, number one, that I was very stubborn. I-- and I had very strong beliefs that you have to deal correctly with people in life, you know? And that's why I'm saying the one slap taught me-- when that woman hit me-- that this is very unjust. And I always felt that justice is very important in the way you deal with people and the way you-- way of thinking, and to, to follow the right path. And just very truthful-- sometimes, I know that, even today, sometimes, if I can't make it. Somebody will invite me. And my husband will say, well, what do you think we should say? And I'll say, the truth. The truth, you know, it's always with me. Well, if I say that I just can't make it because, you know, something else came up. And I'll just tell them. So I don't believe-- you know, I felt that you can persevere as long as you work hard and you, and you, you pray. You hope. You try your best. And, and you know. And that is why I loved America when I came here. Because I felt that you have the freedom to follow these thing, that you don't have anti-Semitism-- at least then, you didn't-- and, and that you can pursue, whether it's schooling, whether it's work, whether it's whatever education, whatever you want to do. I think anybody can do anything, here. So I'm fortunate that things have changed, you know? Thank you very much. Thank you very much for sharing your story. And I would like very much if we could see pictures of your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since we don't have any-- since you don't have any pictures from before the war, would you say your parents' names for us, and then other members of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother and father--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll start, I'll start with my father, was Jacob Kiss. My mother's maiden name was Chaya Lowinger. And my grandmother is-- my mother's mother was Raizel Lowinger. She's the only grandparent that I remember. She's the only one who lived in our city. And then I had an older sister who was about 37 when she was taken to Auschwitz. She had four children. Her name was Leah [? Gutterer. ?] She was married to [? Haskel ?] [? Gutterer ?]. And then I had an older brother whose name was [? Heschel ?] Kiss.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he the one who you found--

speaker: interviewee
text: The one that I found after the liberation. And then he also came to the United States, by the way. He passed away in 1978. And then the sister, my-- the older one of the three-- she was very sick. Her name is Blanche. In Yiddish, we called her [? Blinchen ?]. She was in Bergen-Belsen when they were liberated. She was very, very sick with TB. There was a doctor from our hometown, a lady doctor, who recognized her, and knew who she was, and took very good care of her. And then she went to Sweden, where she stayed for awhile-- a few years. She was there in a sanitarium. And they took care of her. And then she eventually came, in 1951, to the United States. She now lives in New York with her daughter. Her husband passed away. Then I have the sister, whose name is-- actually, the full name was Esther Golda. And her name is now Finested. She is married, has three married children and a whole slew of grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was the sister you were with in the camp [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is the one I was with. And she lives in Toronto. She [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] graduated college, got her master's in teaching, was teaching slow learners, or retarded children, for a while in public school. She's now retired from that. And, and I lived in--

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the brother that looked like Robert Redford?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm so glad you're asking me. Because I was going to talk about him. And, you know, you sit down. And you talk so much. And you get away from the things that really mean a lot. I said that I adored him. Whenever he used to come home, if he just walked in the house, it was like a holiday in our house. He was very good-looking. He had a very good sense of humor. He was singing beautifully. He was just loving to everybody. And I have to talk to him. And I'm so grateful that you [INAUDIBLE] reminded me to talk about him. Because I remember there was an elderly man in our town. And he stopped my mother once. She came home and told us that he said, Mrs. Kiss, I want you to know that you have a son that is born once in 1,000 years. And I mean, he came in. And he would always hug me and kiss me, bring me gifts, and, and just be so full of life. And we just always felt like, when he opened the door, that sunshine came in with him. And his name was Melech, which is translated 'king' in, in English. He was married. He didn't have any children. He was married maybe a year and a half before they took him away. We kept inquiring over and over, everywhere we went. We were just convinced that he had to have survived. And then we met one single person who was in camp with him. He told it to me 50 years ago. I don't remember now exactly which camp he was in. It sounded like one of the very bad ones, where the men were. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Can't think of it now. I happen to have remembered. And this man was telling us a story about him. He said that he was so charismatic that the Germans would come to him all the time, he should sing for them. Again, he was blond, so, you know, he wasn't the typical Jew. But he entertained them. What happened-- it was close before the end of the war. They were also wondering, like everybody else did-- most everybody-- that you went from town to town. And they were very, very hungry. And it's typical of my brother that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that he would try to escape. That was very typical of him, that he would be that kind of a very daring. And it looks like, he said, they were on the road somewhere. And my brother just decided to take off. Because he-- I don't know what he was feeling at the time, whether it was hunger, or, or uncertainty, or what. And he started to run. And they shot him in the back. So I have to conclude, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that as much as, as I mourn my parents, I feel that, for all my life, I've-- he was on top of the list that I mourn the most.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now we will see pictures of your, of your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. OK. This is Renee Klaristenfeld. It's me. It was taken in 1948. I was about 18 years old. It was on the way home from work. I decided to take a picture to send to my sister in Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1948, in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This picture was taken in New York in 1956. My son was five years old. My daughter, Barbara, was four years old. And the baby, Elaine, was, I don't know, maybe two month, three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your son is all the way on the right, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son is on the right. His name is Jack, Jack.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture, I'm sorry, did you say where it was taken? Is this in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said it was taken in New York. I'd be happy to say it again. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Live in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. OK. This picture is of my three children. It was taken at my son's wedding, which took place in New York in 1976. On, on the left of him is Barbara. And to his right is Elaine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Jack-- Yakov.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. These five children are my grandchildren-- my son's children. On the right is David. He is 18. Next to him, in the pink shirt, is Leah. She is 17. Then-- well, the little one in the green shirt is [? Alisherba ?]. She's five years old. The girl standing up next to her is [? Hovah ?]. She is eight years old. And next to her is [? Pinnie ?], who is-- he was 13, is almost 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very recently-- I received it maybe a month ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So probably taken in the end of 1994, maybe, early 1994?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most likely, yes, most likely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and where would this have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somewhere in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The young man is my grandson, my daughter's son. He is 19, born in New York, is still living there. And the little girl's name is [? Simah Tovah ?]. She is four years old, also born in New York and living in New York, Brooklyn, New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- any idea when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I received it also in 1994. So it must have been towards-- sometime in 1994. I have received so many pictures. I don't recall exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my grandson's name is Wagh, W-A-G-H. His first name is [? Asher. ?] And the little girl is [? Simah ?] [? Tovah. ?] Her last name is [? Kerman. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my husband. His name is Michael Klaristenfeld. This picture, I believe, was taken in 1968, in New York.
